coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur Co-doped carbon dots as selective and visual sensors for monitoring cobalt ions
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Lili
Liu, Yaoyang
Wang, Yesheng
Xu, Jiyao
Xiong, Zhong
Zhao, Xihui
Xia, Yanzhi
coredata.dc:description: Using l-cysteine as the precursor, carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms co-doped (N, S-CDs) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method. The doped N and S atoms enhance the fluorescence intensity, and the modified functional group on N, S-CDs facilitate the coordination interaction between N, S-CDs and Co2+. Thus, the prepared N, S-CDs can be used as fluorescence probe to detect Co2+ with excellent selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, various interferences displayed unobvious effect on the sensing of N, S-CDs to Co2+. This probe provided a rapid, visual method to monitor Co2+ with a wide monitoring range to Co2+ (1–50 μM) and a low detection limit of 26 nM. More interestingly, the quenched fluorescence (turn-off) of N, S-CDs induced by Co2+ could be recovered (turn-on) with the addition of H2O2 or EDTA. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is a static quenching, accompanied with electron transfer by forming the complex between Co2+ and functional groups of N, S-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NS co-doped CDs
l-cysteine
Fluorescent probe
Co2+
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of N, S-CDs

Possible mechanism of the fluorescence sensor of N, S-CDs to Co
2+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51503110 ) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41 , No. ZFZ201815 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–C) TEM image, HRTEM image and size distribution of N, S-CDs. (D) AFM image of N, S-CDs. (E) Height profile along the line. (F) Raman spectrum of N, S-CDs.
(A) FT-IR spectrum of N, S-CDs. (B) Full scan XPS spectrum of N, S-CDs. (C–F) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption (red line), FL excitation (black line) and emission spectra (blue line) of N, S-CDs. Inset: N, S-CDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light. (B) FL emission spectra of N, S-CDs with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm in 10 nm increments. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs at excitation wavelength 340 nm in the presence of different metal ions. (B) Fluorescence change ratio F/F0 of N, S-CDs at excitation wavelength 340 nm on addition of different metal ions. (C) Photographic images of N, S-CDs with different metal ions under visible light (top) and UV light (down).

(A) FL emission spectra of N, S-CDs excited at 340 nm with different concentrations of Co2+. (B) Linear relationship between fluorescence change ratio and Co2+ concentration. (C) Photos of N, S-CDs with different concentrations of Co2+ (0–50 μM) under daylight (top) and 365 nm UV lamp (down).

The anti-interference ability of N, S-CDs for Co2+ detection. FL intensity variation (F/F0) of N, S-CDs with Co2+ ([Co2+] = 50 μM, blue bar), different interference metal ions or organic molecules ([interference] = 50 μM, red bar) and upon the subsequent addition of 50 μM Co2+ (green bars). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Photographs of N, S-CDs detection paper with different metal ion (A) and different concentrations of Co2+. (B) under 365 nm UV lamp. The concentration of Co2+ is from 1 to 30 μM. Blank and Control are the filter papers treated and untreated with N, S-CDs, respectively.
(A) The fluorescence decay curves of N, S-CDs without and with Co2+. (B) Zeta potential of N, S-CDs. (C) Full scan XPS spectrum of N, S-CDs after adding Co2+. (D) High-resolution XPS spectra of Co2p.

(A–B) Fluorescence intensity and FL intensity ratio histogram of N, S-CDs (blank), N, S-CDs + Co2+, N, S-CDs + Co2++H2O2 and N, S-CDs + H2O2. (C) FL intensity of N, S-CDs + Co2+ after adding different concentrations of H2O2. (D) The FTIR spectroscopy of (a) N, S-CDs (b) N, S-CDs + Co2+, (C) N, S-CDs + Co2++H2O2.
Schematic diagram of Co2+ sensitive fluorescence detection based on N, S-CDs.

row.entry: nanoparticles based on Schiff base derivative
Cyclic and square-wave anodic stripping voltammetry

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur Co-doped carbon dots as selective and visual sensors for monitoring cobalt ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Second Hospital of Shandong University, Jinan, 250033, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological Textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: L. Sun and Y. Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using l-cysteine as the precursor, carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms co-doped (N, S-CDs) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method. The doped N and S atoms enhance the fluorescence intensity, and the modified functional group on N, S-CDs facilitate the coordination interaction between N, S-CDs and Co2+. Thus, the prepared N, S-CDs can be used as fluorescence probe to detect Co2+ with excellent selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, various interferences displayed unobvious effect on the sensing of N, S-CDs to Co2+. This probe provided a rapid, visual method to monitor Co2+ with a wide monitoring range to Co2+ (1–50 μM) and a low detection limit of 26 nM. More interestingly, the quenched fluorescence (turn-off) of N, S-CDs induced by Co2+ could be recovered (turn-on) with the addition of H2O2 or EDTA. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is a static quenching, accompanied with electron transfer by forming the complex between Co2+ and functional groups of N, S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Most heavy metal ions are toxic and non-degradable, causing serious damage to the environment and the human body [1]. Until now, heavy metal ion pollution has become a major pollutant in industrialized countries [2]. Cobalt ion (Co2+), as an essential trace element in the body, has an important physiological role [3]. It is a component of vitamin B12 and some enzymes, and has a certain influence on the metabolism of some trace elements [4]. However, excessive intake of Co2+ may cause severe heart failure, cardiomyopathy, asthma, thyroid damage and other diseases, which is extremely harmful to the human body [5]. Many countries have made clear regulations on the cobalt ion concentration in domestic water [6]. It is worth noting that cobalt pollution is becoming more and more serious due to mining, industrial wastewater and sewage discharge, medical and scientific research of radioactive experiments, etc. [7]. Therefore, it is very important to develop a rapid, effective and convenient method to detect cobalt ions in the environment [8]. Traditional detection methods include atomic absorption spectrometry [9], electrochemical method [10], fluorescence spectrometry [11] and so on. Among these methods, fluorescence (FL) spectrometry has attracted more and more attention because it does not need expensive equipment and tedious pretreatment, good selectivity and convenient analysis [12].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a typical stable light-emitting carbon nanomaterial [13]. They have great potential in various applications due to their low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, excellent water solubility, surface modification elasticity, photobleaching resistance and excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior [14]. Although plenty of methods have been reported to prepare CDs, hydrothermal method is considered to be a simple, green and effective method [15]. Various materials including man-made materials (such as citric acid, ethylenediamine, folic acid and ascorbic acid [2,3,5,16]) or natural products (such as milk, eggs, flowers, fruits, and leaves) all can be utilized as the carbon source of the hydrothermal method [17–21]. However, the preparation of CDs still faces great challenges, especially due to the poor quantum yield, unstable photoluminescence, and relatively low selectivity and sensitivity as fluorescence sensors. It is worthy to be noted that heteroatoms doping is a very effective method to improve the quantum yield and fluorescence characteristics of CDs [22]. Particularly, N, S co-doped CDs have attracted increasing attentions, in term of the low toxicity, strong chemical inertness, as well as the facile preparation method [23]. Moreover, heteroatoms doping introduces more active sites by surface modification of CDs, which is conducive to enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of detection [24]. In recent years, heteroatoms doped carbon dots as fluorescence sensors have been actively studied in the detection of heavy metal ions [25]. Liao et al. reported nitrogen-doped carbon dots synthesized with citric acid and ethanolamine for detecting Hg2+ in actual water samples rapidly and sensitively [26]. Zhang et al. introduced red/orange N, S co-doped carbon dots with dual-emission for ultrasensitive Ag+ detection [27]. V. Sahu et al. reported boron doped carbon dots for sensitive detection of Pb2+ ions [28]. Nevertheless, there is not much involving the detection of Co2+. Therefore, it is necessary to design a multifunctional fluorescent sensor with high selectivity, high sensitivity and easy operation to detect Co2+ [29].

Cysteine is a common amino acid containing C, N, S elements, which is widely available, cheap and easy to get. It can be used as a potential candidate to synthesize N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) [30]. Herein, l-cysteine derived N, S-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal approach [31]. The prepared N, S-CDs showed excellent stability, water solubility and strong blue fluorescence with relatively high fluorescence quantum yield (QY = 27%). It was further applied to the detection of Co2+, displaying high selectivity and sensitivity. As adding Co2+, the fluorescence of N, S-CDs was quenched due to the chelation of the functional groups modified on N, S-CDs with Co2+, corresponding photoelectron transfer. It is noteworthy that the addition of H2O2 or EDTA can restore the quenched fluorescence of N, S-CDs induced by Co2+. This work offers a great potential for carbon dots as high-performance fluorescent sensors in the detection of metal ions.

l-cysteine was supplied by Love Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (shanghai, China). All metal salts (BaCl2, AlCl3, FeCl3, MgCl2, CaCl2, HgCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, CuCl2, Co(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Na2SO4, NaNO2, NaHCO3, Na2CO3, Na2S2O3, Na2SO4, Na2S, NaH2PO4, Na3PO4, NaNO3, NaF, NaCl, KBr and KI) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Factory (Beijing, China).

Sucrose (Suc), vitamin C (VC), glycine (Gly), glucose (Glc), urea, lysine (Lys), glutathione (GSH), melamine (MIEL), glutamate (Glu), citric acid (CA) and oxalic acid (OA) were purchased from Fuchen (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). All chemical reagents were commercially available analytical grades and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The detail information of the preparation and characterization of N, S-CD was provided in Supplementary Material.
Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was estimated according to literature [32], and the details test method were presented in Supplementary Material.

Co2+ detection was carried out as the following procedure: the prepared N, S-CDs solution (5 mg mL−1) were diluted 50 times with ultra-pure water. The selectivity of N, S-CDs to Co2+ was investigated by adding various substrates in the same way, including metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Al3+ and Fe3+), small organic molecules (GSH, Urea, SUC, Vc, OA, Lys, MIEL, Glu, Gly, Glc, CA) and anion ions (MnO4 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, S2O3 2−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, NO2 −, NO3 −, F−, Cl−, Br−). The final concentration was 50 μM. The FL emission spectra were recorded after reacting a few minutes with 340 nm excitation wavelength and the fluorescence response was analyzed with FL intensity at the maximum emission wavelength. Interference experiments were performed with the coexistent conditions with concentration ratio 1:1 of Co2+ to interference (50 μM).

Sensitive detection was determined by separately adding different concentrations of Co2+ from 0 to 50 μM. After reacting few minutes, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at the same condition and the FL intensity at maximum emission peak was used to quantitatively analyze Co2+ concentration.

The practical application of N, S-CDs to detect Co2+ was evaluated using tap water from our laboratory and lake water from static lake as real water samples. Before testing, the water samples were filtered with 0.22 μm membrane. Standard addition method was performed by adding three known concentration of Co2+ (5, 10, 25 μM), and then the recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated.
The fluorescence recovery of N, S-CDs + Co2+ mixture system was investigated by adding H2O2 (0–90 μM) or EDTA.

Surface morphology of the synthesized N, S-CDs was obtained using TEM and AFM. As evident from Fig. 1 A and Fig. S1, N, S-CDs were well monodispersed with relatively uniform size. The ordered lattice fringes in Fig. 1B with interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm correspond to the graphitic carbon (100) [33]. The average size of them was about 6 nm (Fig. 1C). AFM image in Fig. 1D and E exhibited particle heights of about 6.5 ± 0.5 nm, confirming the monodisperse nanospheres structure of N, S-CDs, which is in good agreement with the results of TEM. Raman spectrum (Fig. 1F) displayed that the disordered (D) and crystalline (G) bands of N, S-CDs appeared at 1353.9 cm−1 and 1565.5 cm−1, respectively [34]. This two special Raman scattering peaks are agree with the existence of sp3 defects and sp2 hybridization of carbon [35].

FT-IR and XPS were performed to obtain the surface chemical component and element states. As shown in the FT-IR spectrum of N, S-CDs (Fig. 2 A), the strong absorption bands around 3414 cm−1 and 3144 cm−1 were attributed to hydroxyl (O–H) and amine (N–H) stretching vibrations, respectively [36], indicating the presence of –OH and –NH2. The peaks at 2932-2987 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 represented the stretching and bending vibration of C–H [37]. Absorbance peak appeared at 2606 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of S–H [38]. The broad absorption peak located at 1708-1593 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of CC, CO and CN in the conjugate structure [39]. The characteristic stretch band of C–N and C–O was found at 1302-1145 cm−1 [40]. The weak peak at 630 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of C–S [36]. It can be declared from the above analysis that N and S atoms had been doped successfully to the CDs and the synthesized N, S-CDs contained nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen functional groups, such as –OH, –SH, –NH2, –COOH [41]. Furthermore, the XPS spectrum (Fig. 2B) displayed four peaks at 164, 286, 399, and 532 eV, representing the binding energy of S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The corresponding atomic content of the four elements of C, O, S, and N were 56.04%, 37.02%, 5.26%, and 1.68%, respectively. The High-resolution spectrum of C1s in Fig. 2C presented three forms of carbon. The peak at 284.7eV corresponded to CC (sp2 carbon), suggesting that N, S-CDs had a graphene structure [2]. The peak at 286.2 eV could be attributed to C–O, C–N, C–S (sp3 carbon), and the peak at 287.7 eV was due to CO/CN [42]. In Fig. 2D, the O1s spectrum showed three peaks at 531.9, 532.5, and 533.2 eV, which may be caused by CO, C–OH/C–O–C and O–CO, respectively [43]. For the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2E), it could be disintegrated into three peaks at 401.5400.3 and 399.5 eV, corresponding to Sp3 hybrid Amino N (–NH2), pyrrolic type N (C3–N) and pyridinic type N (C–N–C, CN), respectively [44]. The S2p high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2F) had an obvious peak at 164.1 eV, which is related to S2p3/2 of thiophene [45]. These results demonstrated that N and S atoms were doped in the framework of CDs and the prepared N, S-CDs possessed a large number of functional groups containing nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen, which are beneficial for improving the water solubility, stability and optical properties of CDs.

UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were performed to investigate the optical properties of the prepared N, S-CDs. UV–vis absorption spectrum displayed a broad absorption peak at 270 nm (Fig. 3 A), which should belong to π-π⁎ transition of CC bonds (sp2 carbon) [2]. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectrum exhibited the optimum excitation and emission peaks at 340 nm and 420 nm, respectively. The aqueous solution of N, S-CDs was dark yellow under sunlight, while emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (the insert of Fig. 3A). In addition, the FL emission spectra of N, S-CDs were also investigated under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3B). It could be observed that the maximum emission wavelength shifted to longer wavelength when the excitation wavelength changed from 300 to 400 nm, and the strongest emission spectrum occurred at 340 nm excitation wavelength, which demonstrated the excitation dependent emission behavior of N, S-CDs. Additionally, the fluorescence stability was further evaluated. As shown in Fig. S2, the FL emission intensity of N, S-CDs did not change obviously with the addition of NaCl (0–2 mol/L) or exposing to 365 nm UV light for 0–2 h, demonstrating strong salt tolerance and light stability N, S-CDs.

As a fluorescent sensor, it is very significant for practical applications to explore the selectivity and sensitivity of N, S-CDs to analytes. Fig. 4 A displayed the FL emission spectra of N, S-CDs with various metal ions. It could be clearly observed that except Co2+, other metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+ and Fe3+) had little influence to the FL intensity of N, S-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence change ratio (Fig. 4B) with the addition of each metal ion further demonstrated the remarkable effect of Co2+ to N, S-CDs, where F0 and F represent the FL intensity of N, S-CDs before (blank) and after adding metal ions, respectively. Meanwhile, the photographs of N, S-CDs with different metal ions under daylight and 365 nm UV light were provided in Fig. 4C. The color of N, S-CDs turned from light yellow to brown with the addition of Co2+, while without significant color change after adding other metal ions. Moreover, the fluorescence of N, S-CDs under UV light was obviously quenched only with Co2+. In addition, as depicted in Fig. S3, most anions had little effect on the FL intensity of N, S-CDs. All these indicated the high selectivity of N, S-CDs for the determination of Co2+. The selectivity to Co2+ may be due to the Co2+ could react with the groups on the surface of N, S-CDs, forming a complex, which facilitate electron transfer from the excited state of N, S-CDs to the 3d orbital of Co2+, resulting in the fluorescence quenching.

The sensitivity of N, S-CDs to Co2+ was investigated (Fig. 5 ) by adding different concentration of Co2+. As shown in Fig. 5A, the FL emission intensity of N, S-CDs declined gradually when the Co2+ concentration increased from 0 to 50 μM, indicating that the fluorescence of N, S-CDs was gradually quenched. The plot of fluorescence change ratio (F/F0) vs. Co2+ concentration (Fig. 5B) presented a good linear relationship between them (F/F0 = 0.0118C + 0.98), and the linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.992, where F0 and F represent the FL intensities of N, S-CDs without and with Co2+, respectively. The lowest detection limit (LOD) was estimated to be 26 nM according to LOD = 3σ/K [40]. Interestingly, the color of N, S-CDs gradually became darker under daylight and the fluorescence gradually tended to weaker under 365 nm UV lamp as the concentration of Co2+ increased, which could be observed with naked eyes (Fig. 5C). The results demonstrated that the N, S-CDs sensor was sensitive to Co2+, and could be applied as fluorescent sensor for the efficient detection of Co2+.

In order to determine the practicality of N, S-CDs as fluorescent sensors, it is meaningful to investigate their anti-interference ability in complex practical systems. Various metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+) and small organic molecules (GSH, Urea, SUC, Vc, OA, Lys, MIEL, Glu, Gly, Glc, CA) were explored as interferences. As shown in Fig. 6 , the interference provided unobvious effect on the fluorescence of N, S-CDs. More importantly, when the interference and Co2+ were added together, the fluorescence of N, S-CDs remained quenching, indicating that most interferences can coexist with Co2+ in the same analytical system without affecting the detection of N, S-CDs to Co2+. This result further proved the feasibility of N, S-CDs to detect Co2+.

In recent years, many methods for detecting Co2+ ions have been reported, such as electrochemistry, colorimetry, atomic absorption spectrometry, fluorescence spectroscopy, and some were shown in Table 1 . However, most of them exist some limitations, such as narrow detection range, high detection limit, long response time, and ion inference effect. Compared with other methods, our proposed fluorescence sensing probe have the advantages of comparable or even better detection limit and wide linear range, simple and convenient detection method and low cost. Moreover, the N, S-CDs could be used directly without dialysis purification. The selectivity of N, S-CDs after purification was presented in Fig. S4. Fluorescence change ratio F/F0 of N, S-CDs to Co2+ changed from 0.32 (Fig. 4B) to 0.59 (Fig. S4), indicating the selectivity to Co2+ decreased.

The practical application of N, S-CDs was further checked to monitor Co2+ in real water samples. Standard addition method was performed by adding three known concentration of Co2+ (5, 10, 25 μM) into tap water and lake water. The data was calculated according to the previously obtained standard curve (Fig. 5B). As shown in Table S1, the recoveries for Co2+ detection ranged from 98.40% to 106.00%, as well as the relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 3.95%, illustrating that the proposed test method was relatively accurate for detecting Co2+ in environmental water samples.

Additionally, in order to detect Co2+ more conveniently and quickly without using special instruments, N, S-CDs detection paper was prepared by immersing the filter paper in N, S-CDs aqueous solution and then air dried. The prepared paper-based sensors displayed blue fluorescence and could be utilized for the detection of Co2+. As shown in Fig. 7 A, when different metal ion (1 mM) was dropped on the test paper and exposed to UV light (365 nm), only Co2+ caused fluorescence quenching of the N, S-CDs detection paper. More importantly, when different concentrations of Co2+ (1–30 μM) were dropped on the test paper, the fluorescence color change under UV light could be clearly observed (Fig. 7B), indicating the paper sensor is extremely sensitive to Co2+. Therefore, this method provides an efficient and simple way for the determination of Co2+, especially the water quality check in developing countries without complex detection devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Possible mechanism of the fluorescence sensor of N, S-CDs to Co
2+
ce:section.ce:para:
It has been reported that CDs can produce different fluorescence responses to analytes according to different mechanisms, such as static quenching, dynamic quenching, electron transfer, internal filtering effect (IFE), etc. [24,40,52]. In order to clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism associated with N, S-CDs, a variety of analysis techniques (fluorescence lifetime, XPS, zeta potential, cyclic voltammetry curve analysis) were implemented. Time-resolved fluorescence decay is a significant parameter to determine the static and dynamic quenching process. The fluorescence decay lifetime curves of N, S-CDs without and with Co2+ (60 μM) were shown in Fig. 8 A. Fitted the double exponential function y (t) to get the lifetime decay curve, and calculate the average life accordingly [53].
y
( t )
=
A 1
e
− t
τ 1
+
A 2
e
− t
τ 2
τ* =
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

The average fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CDs before and after adding Co2+ were 3.42 and 3.14 ns, suggesting it is a static quenching progress. Besides, the zeta potential of N, S-CDs (−48.3 eV) changed to −24.7eV after the addition of Co2+ (Fig. 8B), this might be due to the deprotonated functional groups (including carboxyl, amino and thiol groups) of N, S-CDs, which can interact with Co2+ ions through metal ligand interactions [5]. XPS was further performed to verify the interaction between N, S-CDs and Co2+ (Fig. 8C). Compared with the XPS spectrum of N, S-CDs in Fig. 2B, a new peak appeared at 779 eV after adding Co2+, which was attributed to Co2p. The Co2p spectrum (Fig. 8D) had two main peaks at 796.3 eV and 779 eV due to Co2p1/2 and Co2p3/2 spin-orbit binding energy of Co–O. And the three satellite peaks appeared at 780.8 eV, 786 eV and 802.1 eV are characteristic peaks of the Co–O phase [54]. Moreover, the C1s, O1s, N1s and S2p spectrum were all changed slightly (Fig. S5). These results further proved that Co2+ reacted with N, S-CDs and caused the changes of groups on the surface of N, S-CDs.

In addition, we assumed that a complex was formed involving in the strong binding affinity between the functional groups of N, S -CDs and Co2+, which facilitate electron transfer from the excited state of N, S-CDs to the 3d orbital of Co2+, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. The energy levels of E LUMO and E HOMO of N, S-CDs were estimated with the following empirical formulas [55]:
E LOMO
= − e
(
E Red
+ 4.4 )
E HOMO
= − e
(
E Ox
+ 4.4 )
E HOMO
=
E LOMO
−
E g
where E LUMO and E HOMO are the energy of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital and the highest occupied molecular orbital, respectively. E Red and E ox are the reduction and oxidation potential of N, S-CDs, respectively. The E Red was determined to be −0.31 eV (Fig. S6A), and the ELOMO was −4.09 eV. E HOMO can be determined by combining E LOMO with the optical band gap (E g). The value of E g was estimated to be 4.80 eV (Fig. S6B) [56], the corresponding E HOMO was −8.89 eV. When Co2+ was added, it could react with N, S-CDs by chelating with sulfhydryl, amino, hydroxyl groups and carboxyl on the surface of N, S-CDs, as well as the excited state electrons of N, S-CDs transfer to the 3d orbital of Co2+. This sensing progress inhibit the fluorescence emission electrons transition, inducing the fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence quenching mechanism induced by Co2+ was described in Fig. S6C and Scheme 1 .

According to the research, the strong oxidizing properties of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) may enable the N, S-CDs-based fluorescence system to be a good candidate as “on-off-on” fluorescent switch sensor for monitoring H2O2 [45]. As shown in Fig. 9 A &B, after adding a certain amount of H2O2 (9 μM) for a period of time, it was intuitively exhibited that the FL intensity of N, S-CDs + Co2+ have recovered. However, adding the same concentration of H2O2 alone had no significant effect on the FL intensity of N, S-CDs. In order to determine the influence of H2O2 concentration on the fluorescence recovery, different concentrations of H2O2 (0–90 μM) were added to the N, S-CDs + Co2+ system. It could be seen from Fig. 9C that the FL intensity of the system restored gradually with the increase of H2O2 concentration. The fluorescence recovery mechanism of H2O2 to the N, S-CDs + Co2+ may be due to the strong oxidation ability. Fig. 9D presented the infrared spectrum of N, S-CDs + Co2+ with H2O2. It could be seen that a peak appears at 1120 cm−1 after the addition of H2O2, which is the CS stretching vibration absorption peak [57]. This is due to the strong oxidizing properties of H2O2, which breaks the bond between Co2+ and sulfhydryl, amino, and hydroxyl groups, and sulfhydryl is oxidized to CS. The effect of Co2+ on N, S-CDs disappeared, leading to the fluorescence turned on. The possible mechanism of N, S-CDs as “on-off-on” fluorescent switch sensor was presented in Scheme 1.

More interestingly, it was found that EDTA could also restore the FL of N, S-CDs + Co2+. EDTA is a famous chelating agent that has excellent ability to form stable complexes with metal ions [20]. EDTA did not affect the FL of N, S-CDs. While, the FL of N, S-CDs + Co2+ system was recovered after the addition of EDTA (Fig. S7A &B). Moreover, the brown color produced by the reaction of Co2+ and N, S-CDs disappeared and the fluorescence was turned on again under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S7C). The possible reason is that EDTA can chelate with Co2+ to form a more stable Co2+-EDTA complex, inducing the influence of Co2+ on N, S-CDs diminish, as well as the quenched fluorescence recovery.

In summary, l-cysteine derived N, S-CDs were prepared via a simple and green one-step hydrothermal method. N and S atoms were successfully doped into the framework of the CDs, and functional groups were modified on N, S-CDs, which were related to the strong fluorescence, high water solubility, excellent stability and excitation-dependent behavior. The prepared N, S-CDs was further applied for monitoring Co2+ as fluorescence sensor, exhibiting excellent selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference ability. This fluorescence sensor offered a rapid, visual and feasible method for quantitative detection of Co2+ with a wide linear response range (1–50 μM) and a low detection limit of 26 nM. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is a combination of static quenching and excited state photoelectron transfer by forming the complex between Co2+ and functional groups of N, S-CDs. Moreover, this method was utilized to analysis Co2+ in real water samples, confirming its high potential in Co2+ sensing with high accuracy, simple and quick procedure. More interestingly, the quenched fluorescence (turn-off) of N, S-CDs induced by Co2+ could be turned on by adding H2O2 or EDTA.

Lili Sun: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yaoyang Liu: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yesheng Wang: Formal analysis. Jiyao Xu: Formal analysis. Zhong Xiong: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xihui Zhao: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yanzhi Xia: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51503110) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41, No. ZFZ201815).

Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhao, S. Zou, D. Huo, C. Hou, M. Yang, J. Li, M. Bian, Simple and sensitive fluorescence sensor for methotrexate detection based on the inner filter effect of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots, Anal. Chim. Acta. 1047 (2019) 179-187.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High fluorescence S, N co-doped carbon dots as an ultra-sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of uric acid
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, Q. Lu, Y. Hou, Y. Liu, Y. Zhang, High fluorescence S, N co-doped carbon dots as an ultra-sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of uric acid, Talanta. 155 (2016) 62-69.
